fixture_purpose: UK cohabiting couple separated 8 months ago, Consolidation life stage (age 50 — anchor archetype 26). Was cohabiting 12 years; NOT married. Exercises TOLATA property division in pending-dispute state, absence of spousal exemption on IHT, absence of transferable NRB/RNRB, no PSO rights (cohabitants cannot claim pension-sharing on separation), stale life-insurance nomination (ex-cohabiting partner still named), unprotected pension path on separation, and informal child custody arrangement. Highlights how cohabitants are significantly disadvantaged vs married couples in UK law — key engine output differentiator.

individual.name: Mr T Cohab
individual.legal_status_note: Cohabitants have no automatic financial rights on separation under English law; no right to claim against partner's pension; no spousal IHT exemption; no transferable NRB

ex_partner_entity.name: Ms T Cohab (ex)
ex_partner_entity.reason_retained: Joint property ownership (TOLATA); shared child custody; stale nomination reference

relationships.pso_note: Cohabitants have no right to pension-sharing under Matrimonial Causes Act 1973; no court order possible without marriage

children.name: Child T Cohab
children.custody_note: No formal court order; informal 60/40 arrangement; ex-partner primary carer; no CMS assessment yet

property_disputes.dispute_basis: Both parties claim 50/50 beneficial interest; no cohabitation agreement in place; dispute centres on contribution differentials during relationship
property_disputes.engine_note: Property value uncertain pending TOLATA outcome; engine shows property at full market value with 50% share but flags TOLATA-pending; net worth range reflects possible outcomes 0-100% of 50% share after dispute costs

nomination.stale_reason: Ex-cohabiting partner named; relationship ended 8 months ago; engine flags: 'Pension nomination names ex-partner — update required'; no legal bar to keeping this but strong recommendation to update

pensions.pso_note: Pension is 100% this individual's; ex-partner has no claim under UK law for cohabitants

property.engine_note: Property counted at 50% of market value (£210k) for this individual's net worth; TOLATA outcome may reduce to 0% or increase up to 100% after costs; wide confidence range

liabilities.joint_liability_note: Both parties remain liable for this mortgage despite separation; both names on deeds; TOLATA outcome will determine how resolved

liabilities.note: Renting while TOLATA dispute unresolved; double-housing-cost situation — both paying mortgage AND rent

liabilities.null_reason: FP-4 coverage: litigation costs uncertain; range £8,000–£25,000 depending on dispute complexity; engine models mid-range estimate in net worth confidence band

liabilities.note: Accumulated post-separation; covering rental costs and legal retainer

nomination.stale_reason: Ex-cohabiting partner named on free-standing nomination; relationship ended; not automatically removed unlike spousal nominations in some jurisdictions; must be actively updated

events.note: Left shared property; ex-partner remains in occupation

events.engine_action: APQ active: chase TOLATA outcome; update property net worth when resolved

fp4.expected_behaviour: TOLATA pending — property beneficial interest uncertain; engine shows 50% share as current value but flags wide range; net worth confidence = low for property component; scenario outputs: 0% share (loss), 50% (status quo), 100% (win)
Legal costs uncertain; engine uses £15k mid-range estimate as committed liability in net worth calculation; flags as estimated; range in output
Informal custody — no CMS assessment; engine flags: potential maintenance liability if ex-partner applies; cashflow engine adds sensitivity note not a committed outflow

fp5.expected_behaviour: Stale pension nomination on ex-cohabiting partner; engine generates high-priority APQ; unlike divorce (automatic nomination invalidation not applicable for cohabitants); pension could pass to ex-partner on death if not updated
Stale life policy nomination on ex-cohabiting partner; engine generates high-priority APQ; policy not in trust so proceeds could pass to estate or named ex-partner depending on policy terms

key_legal_distinctions_vs_married: No right to claim against ex-partner pension (no PSO possible)
No automatic spousal IHT exemption on death
No transferable NRB — £325k only (vs up to £650k for married couple)
No transferable RNRB — direct descendant qualifier works but no doubling from partner
TOLATA required for property division (vs automatic MCA 1973 powers for married)

iht_position_vs_married_equivalent.note: If dies before TOLATA resolves and before updating nominations, estate worse off vs married equivalent by potentially £325k transferable NRB

archetype_specific.cohabitation_agreement_apq: No cohabitation agreement was in place — lesson learned; APQ: consider deed of trust / cohabitation agreement if entering new relationship

finio_score.confidence_reason: TOLATA property value uncertain; stale nominations on both pension and life policy; double housing costs compress cashflow score; IHT position structurally weaker than married equivalent

capital.note: TOLATA outcome drives wide range on property component

protection.note: Policies in place but both stale nominations; no trust structure

cashflow.note: Double housing costs; mortgage + rent; credit card building

debt.note: Joint mortgage liability continues; rental commitment; legal costs estimate pending

estate.note: No spousal exemption; no transferable NRB; stale nominations — structurally weakest dimension

net_worth.note: Wide range entirely driven by TOLATA property outcome

iht_projection.confidence_reason: Property outcome uncertain; no spousal exemption; no transferable NRB makes mid-scenario estate marginally exposed
iht_projection.structural_note: Same wealth level as mrT-couple would have zero IHT exposure (£650k combined NRB); cohabiting status alone creates IHT disadvantage

cost_of_inaction.explanation: Primary: TOLATA delay — each month unresolved costs double housing costs (~£1,920/mo = £23k/yr). Secondary: stale nominations risk pension/life proceeds going to ex-partner. Tertiary: no spousal exemption means IHT planning gap.
